body:
After #20, the orchestrator agent becomes more powerful for handling a single task. But they are constrained inside one PR and cannot be used to coordinate multiple PRs or issues. How to support that? 

@sepo-agent /answer think about a plan for this?

comments.body:
I think the right direction is to add a **tracking-issue orchestrator** above the existing single-target orchestrator, rather than stretching the current PR/issue handoff loop until it becomes ambiguous.

Current architecture I found:

- [`agent-router.yml`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.github/workflows/agent-router.yml) is the entry point that resolves mentions/labels into routes and dispatches built-in workflows.
- [`agent-orchestrator.yml`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.github/workflows/agent-orchestrator.yml) is currently a post-action workflow dispatcher.
- [`handoff.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/handoff.ts) encodes the bounded state machine: `implement -> review -> fix-pr -> review`.
- [`orchestrate-handoff.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/cli/orchestrate-handoff.ts) handles dedupe markers and dispatches the next route-specific workflow.
- [`dispatch-agent-orchestrator.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/cli/dispatch-agent-orchestrator.ts) is the small bridge from action workflows back to the orchestrator.
- The docs in [`agent-orchestrator.md`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/docs/technical-details/agent-orchestrator.md) describe the orchestrator as a bounded post-action handoff layer, not a cross-issue project coordinator.

So after #20, the single-target orchestrator should stay focused: one issue or PR, bounded loop, explicit opt-in. For “longer more complex tasks,” I’d introduce a separate concept: **multi-target orchestration from a tracking issue**.

Proposed model:

- A user creates or asks Sepo to create a tracking issue, e.g. “Epic: support larger tasks.”
- The tracking issue owns a durable task graph in a hidden marker/comment, not in one workflow run.
- The graph contains child tasks with status, dependencies, target issue/PR numbers, assigned route, and current run state.
- The multi-target orchestrator wakes up on `/orchestrate` or `agent/orchestrate`, reads the tracking issue graph, picks ready tasks, and dispatches existing workflows.
- Each child task still uses today’s normal bounded flow: `implement`, `review`, `fix-pr`, etc.
- Completion of child PRs updates the tracking issue graph and may enqueue the next dependent child.
- The tracking issue becomes the coordination surface; child issues/PRs remain small and reviewable.

Implementation approach aligned with current patterns:

1. Add a new route mode for tracking issues, probably reusing `/orchestrate` but detecting an “orchestration plan” marker on the issue. If no marker exists, the agent can draft one and ask for approval.
2. Add a typed task-graph schema under `.agent/src`, for example:
   - task id
   - title
   - route: `implement | review | fix-pr | answer`
   - target kind/number
   - dependencies
   - status: `planned | ready | running | blocked | done | failed`
   - last workflow run id
   - result URL
3. Store the graph in the tracking issue as a hidden marker plus a visible summary comment. This matches the existing marker style used by handoff dedupe in [`handoff.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/handoff.ts).
4. Add a new CLI, e.g. `.agent/src/cli/orchestrate-plan.ts`, that:
   - reads the tracking issue
   - validates the graph
   - expires stale `running` tasks
   - selects ready tasks within a configurable concurrency limit
   - dispatches existing workflows through the same `dispatchWorkflow` helper in [`github.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/github.ts)
   - writes updated task state back to the tracking issue
5. Keep permissions explicit. The multi-target orchestrator must re-check route authorization for each delegated action, not just authorize `/orchestrate` once.
6. Add configuration:
   - `AGENT_ORCHESTRATION_MAX_ACTIVE_TASKS`, default `1`
   - `AGENT_ORCHESTRATION_MAX_CHILD_ISSUES`, default maybe `5`
   - reuse existing max-rounds for each child task
7. Add tests around graph parsing, ready-task selection, dependency blocking, stale run recovery, dedupe, and route authorization.
8. Update docs to separate:
   - single-target orchestration: bounded action chain for one issue/PR
   - tracking orchestration: durable project coordination across multiple issues/PRs

Suggested execution plan:

1. Land/finish #20 as the explicit single-target orchestrator baseline.
2. Add the tracking issue schema and parser with tests only.
3. Add read-only `/orchestrate` behavior that summarizes a plan without dispatching.
4. Add approved dispatch of one ready child task at a time.
5. Add child task completion updates back into the tracking issue.
6. Add bounded concurrency only after the single-task path is stable.
7. Document the UX with examples:
   - `/orchestrate create a plan`
   - `/orchestrate run next`
   - `/orchestrate status`
   - `/orchestrate pause`

I would not make one agent run own multiple PR branches directly. The safer shape is: one durable tracking issue coordinates many normal, bounded Sepo runs. That keeps review surfaces clean, preserves GitHub-native auditability, and avoids turning a single workflow run into a long-lived project manager.

After merging #34 how should we implement this? Also is it related to the latest issue? 

@sepo-agent /answer

After #34, I’d implement #33 as a **tracking-issue orchestrator that consumes project-management signals**, not as an extension of the project manager itself.

Small correction: #34 is the closed issue “Agent for project management”; it was implemented by PR #39 and main now has the phase-one project manager in [`agent-project-manager.yml`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.github/workflows/agent-project-manager.yml), with managed `priority/*` + `effort/*` label application through [`project-management-labels.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/project-management-labels.ts) and [`apply-project-management-labels.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/cli/apply-project-management-labels.ts).

So the shape should be:

- #34 project manager: repo-wide signal layer.
  - Scores/summarizes open issues and PRs.
  - Optionally applies `priority/*` and `effort/*`.
  - Does **not** dispatch implementation work.

- #33 tracking orchestrator: execution layer.
  - Owns one tracking issue / project plan.
  - Maintains a task graph.
  - Dispatches existing workflows (`implement`, `review`, `fix-pr`) one child task at a time.
  - Uses #34 labels as input when deciding what is ready or important.

Concrete implementation plan for #33 after #34:

1. Add a tracking-plan schema under `.agent/src`, with fields like:
   - task id
   - title
   - target issue/PR number
   - dependencies
   - status: `planned | ready | running | blocked | done | failed`
   - priority/effort snapshot
   - last workflow run id/result URL

2. Store the plan on the tracking issue using a hidden marker/comment, similar to the existing handoff marker pattern in [`handoff.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/handoff.ts).

3. Add a CLI, probably `.agent/src/cli/orchestrate-plan.ts`, that:
   - reads the tracking issue
   - parses/validates the plan
   - refreshes child issue/PR state and labels
   - selects the next ready task
   - dispatches existing workflows using the same workflow-dispatch helper used by [`orchestrate-handoff.ts`](https://github.com/self-evolving/repo/blob/main/.agent/src/cli/orchestrate-handoff.ts)
   - updates the tracking issue with visible status

4. Keep execution conservative:
   - default max active tasks: `1`
   - explicit route authorization per delegated task
   - dedupe markers per child dispatch
   - stale `running` recovery
   - visible stop reasons

5. Wire it through `/orchestrate` only when the target issue has a tracking-plan marker. Otherwise, current single-target behavior should remain:
   - issue -> `implement`
   - open PR -> `review` or `fix-pr`
   - bounded follow-up chain

6. Add docs separating:
   - project manager: labels/summaries
   - single-target orchestrator: one issue/PR chain
   - tracking orchestrator: multi-issue execution plan

This is related to the latest issue #43, but #43 is a narrower bug/UX gap: `/orchestrate` on a merged/closed PR should create new branch-change work instead of stopping with `pull request is merged`.

I’d treat #43 as adjacent and probably implement it **before** the full #33 tracking orchestrator. It gives us a useful primitive for #33: turning a follow-up request from a closed/merged PR into a fresh issue or fresh implementation run against `main`.

Recommended order:

1. Implement #43: closed/merged PR `/orchestrate` creates a tracking issue or dispatches fresh implementation work, with a link-back comment.
2. Implement #33 phase 1: tracking issue schema + read-only status summary.
3. Implement #33 phase 2: dispatch one ready child task.
4. Implement #33 phase 3: consume #34 labels for task selection.

So yes, #43 is related, but it is not the full multi-PR orchestrator. It is the first concrete edge case that pushes `/orchestrate` beyond “current open target only,” and it should feed into the same design rather than becoming a separate orchestration path.

title: orchestrate longer more complex tasks
